questions.question: Choose the correct verb: "All of his books _____ worth reading."
questions.explanation: A plural subject ('books') takes the plural verb 'are.'

questions.question: Choose the correct verb: "A lot of work _____ to be done."
questions.explanation: When 'a lot of' is followed by an uncountable noun like 'work,' the verb is singular.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb: "A lot of people _____ in ghosts."
questions.explanation: When 'a lot of' is followed by a plural noun like 'people,' the verb is plural.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb: "I _____ Tokyo last summer."
questions.explanation: 'Last summer' signals simple past tense; 'visited' is the past form.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb: "Every morning I _____ the paper."
questions.explanation: Habits and routines use simple present tense.

questions.question: Choose the correct verb: "When I saw him he _____ writing a letter."
questions.explanation: 'Saw' is past simple, so the second verb takes past continuous 'was writing.'

questions.question: Add the correct punctuation: "We had a great time in France ___ the kids really enjoyed it."

choices.A: no punctuation, leave as one sentence
choices.D: semicolon after 'France' with no other change

questions.explanation: These are two complete thoughts; separate them with a period and start the next thought with a capital letter to avoid a run-on.

questions.question: Add the correct punctuation: "Some people work best in the mornings ___ others do better in the evenings."
questions.explanation: Two related independent clauses joined without a conjunction take a semicolon.

questions.question: Rewrite this sentence with correct capitalization: "i met john at the post office"

choices.A: I met john at the post office.
choices.B: I met John at the post office.
choices.C: i met John at the post office.
choices.D: I Met John At The Post Office.

questions.explanation: Capitalize the first word of a sentence ('I') and proper nouns ('John'). 'Post office' is a common noun and is not capitalized.

questions.question: Rewrite this sentence with correct capitalization: "chinese is difficult to learn"
questions.explanation: Capitalize the first word of a sentence and proper nouns including languages ('Chinese').

questions.question: Rewrite this sentence with correct capitalization: "miss sophia is my teacher"
questions.explanation: Capitalize the title ('Miss') and the proper noun ('Sophia'), plus the first word of the sentence.

questions.question: What is the full form of the underlined word? "He's just gone out."
questions.explanation: In this sentence 'He's' stands for 'He has' because of the perfect-tense 'gone.'

questions.question: What is the full form of the underlined word? "It's raining."
questions.explanation: 'It's' = 'It is.' (Note: 'its' without an apostrophe is the possessive form.)

questions.question: What is the full form of the underlined word? "I can't give an explanation."
questions.explanation: 'Can't' is the contraction of 'cannot' (one word).

questions.question: Choose the correct conjunction: "He must be punished _____ he is guilty."
questions.explanation: 'Because' introduces a reason (subordinating conjunction).

questions.question: Choose the correct conjunction: "I waited for him _____ the clock struck seven."
questions.explanation: 'Until' shows the time something stopped happening.

questions.question: Choose the correct conjunction: "_____ you do not apologize, I shall punish you."
questions.explanation: 'If' introduces a condition that must be met (subordinating conjunction).

questions.question: Which is the best way to fix this run-on sentence: "We had a great time in France the kids really enjoyed it"?

choices.A: Leave it as it is — it reads fine.
choices.B: We had a great time in France, the kids really enjoyed it.
choices.C: We had a great time in France. The kids really enjoyed it.
choices.D: We had a great time in France the kids, really enjoyed it.

questions.explanation: Two complete thoughts must be separated with a period (or a semicolon or a comma + conjunction). A comma alone (choice B) creates a comma splice, another type of run-on.

questions.question: Which is the best way to fix this run-on: "Some people work best in the mornings others do better in the evenings"?

choices.A: Some people work best in the mornings, others do better in the evenings.
choices.B: Some people work best in the mornings; others do better in the evenings.
choices.C: Some, people work best in the mornings others do better, in the evenings.
choices.D: Some people work best, in the mornings others do better in the evenings.

questions.explanation: A semicolon correctly joins two related independent clauses. Choice A is a comma splice.

questions.question: Choose the correct pronoun: "We are going to the movies. Would you like to come with _____?"
questions.explanation: After the preposition 'with,' use the object pronoun 'us.'

questions.question: Choose the correct pronoun: "Diana is one player _____ may beat me."
questions.explanation: 'Who' is the subject of the verb 'may beat' in the relative clause.

questions.question: Choose the correct pronoun: "An argument developed between his sister and _____."
questions.explanation: After the preposition 'between,' use the object pronoun 'him.'
